text: The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the gastroesophageal junction. The left crux is dissected off the angle of His, and the pars flaccida is opened to expose the right crux. No hiatal hernia is observed. The left gastric artery is identified, and the lesser sac is centered inferiorly to it. A four-centimeter mass is visible in the lesser curvature, approximately two centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction, bulging towards the upper edge of the pancreas.

An intraoperative endoscopy confirms the presence of the lesion seen on MRI and endoscopic ultrasound. The surgeon carefully enucleates the lesion from the surrounding gastric tissues using robotic assistance.
The surgeon encounters an area where the tumor is firmly adherent to the stomach mucosa. To facilitate removal of the tumor with its capsule, the mucosa is carefully opened.

After completing the enucleation, the surgeon sutures the mucosa with 3-0 PDS sutures and re-approximates the muscular layer using 2-0 VLOG sutures. Hemostasis is confirmed, and an intraoperative endoscopy shows no leaks, with the gastroesophageal junction visibly wide open.
